hotspots.reason: This is the O(ni*nj*nk) GEMM update performing the vast majority of floating-point operations and memory traffic; dominates runtime for any non-trivial sizes.
Touches the entire C matrix once (O(ni*nj)) and adds extra memory bandwidth demand; smaller than the GEMM update but can be noticeable for small nk.

hotspots.location: print_array(): full matrix dump (i-j with fprintf)
hotspots.estimated_time_percentage: 0-95% (depends on whether dumping is enabled and output target)
hotspots.reason: If POLYBENCH_DUMP_* is enabled, formatted I/O can dominate runtime and completely mask kernel performance; otherwise it is typically compiled out or redirected.

bottleneck_type.reasoning: The i-k-j ordering streams B[k][j] and updates C[i][j] for every k, causing repeated loads/stores of C and poor cache reuse of B across i. Without blocking, arithmetic intensity is reduced by excessive memory traffic (especially C read-modify-write each k), making performance largely limited by memory bandwidth and cache behavior rather than peak FLOPs. If sizes fit well in cache, it can become more compute-bound, but the naive loop order typically makes it memory-dominated for realistic NI/NJ/NK.

gpu_suitability.reasoning: This is a classic dense GEMM (highly parallel, regular memory access when properly tiled) and maps extremely well to GPUs. Naive CPU-style loops are not ideal, but using a GPU GEMM implementation achieves very high utilization.
gpu_suitability.recommended_approach: Use cuBLAS/rocBLAS gemm (e.g., cublasGemmEx/cublasSgemm/Dgemm) with device-resident A/B/C. Transfer A/B/C to GPU once, run GEMM, and copy back C if needed. If implementing manually, use 2D thread blocks, shared-memory tiling for A and B, and accumulate C in registers; fuse beta scaling into the kernel.

validation.reasoning: The initial analysis correctly identifies the memory bottleneck due to poor cache reuse in the i-k-j loop order. This order results in frequent reloading of matrix B and inefficient use of cache for matrix C, leading to high memory traffic and limited performance by memory bandwidth.

detailed_optimizations.code_changes: Introduce loop tiling to improve cache reuse. Change the loop order to i-j-k and apply tiling on the i and j loops.
Unroll the innermost loop (j-loop) to reduce loop overhead and increase instruction-level parallelism.
Use compiler pragmas or intrinsics to vectorize the innermost loop operations.
Use preprocessor directives to conditionally compile the print_array function only when needed.
detailed_optimizations.expected_speedup: Negligible in computation, but reduces unnecessary I/O overhead.

gpu_implementation.strategy: Use a tiled approach with shared memory to maximize data reuse. Each thread block computes a tile of the output matrix C.
gpu_implementation.data_layout: Store matrices in row-major order and use shared memory for tiles of A and B to reduce global memory accesses.
gpu_implementation.expected_speedup: 10x-50x depending on GPU architecture and problem size

performance_analysis.arithmetic_intensity: 2 * NI * NJ * NK / (NI * NJ + NI * NK + NK * NJ) FLOPs/byte
performance_analysis.cache_behavior: The current loop order results in poor cache reuse for matrices B and C. Tiling and reordering can significantly improve cache locality.
data_dependencies: C[i][j] depends on previous C[i][j] values
A[i][k] and B[k][j] are independent across iterations
